claim: On June 5, 1967, Israel launched a preemptive air strike against Egyptian airfields, opening the Six-Day War. By the war's end on June 10, Israel had taken the West Bank and East Jerusalem from Jordan, the Gaza Strip and the Sinai Peninsula from Egypt, and the Golan Heights from Syria — roughly tripling the territory under Israeli control. An additional approximately 300,000 Palestinians were displaced from the West Bank and Gaza, and tens of thousands of Syrians from the Golan. U.N. Security Council Resolution 242 (November 22, 1967) called for the “withdrawal of Israeli armed forces from territories occupied in the recent conflict.” Israel withdrew from the Sinai under the 1979 Egypt-Israel peace treaty (the Camp David Accords); the Golan was unilaterally annexed under the 1981 Golan Heights Law (an annexation not recognized by the international community); the West Bank, East Jerusalem, and Gaza remain under Israeli occupation, settlement expansion, or blockade as of 2026.

sources.label: United Nations Security Council Resolution 242 (November 22, 1967) — full text.
Avi Shlaim, The Iron Wall: Israel and the Arab World (W. W. Norton, updated 2014).
Michael B. Oren, Six Days of War: June 1967 and the Making of the Modern Middle East (Oxford University Press, 2002).
United Nations OCHA — “Occupied Palestinian Territory: Humanitarian context” reference page.

notes: Shlaim and Oren are the canonical modern histories from opposing analytical perspectives — both used in university survey courses. UNSCR 242 is the governing international legal text on the post-war territorial settlement. The 1981 Golan Heights Law is at https://www.knesset.gov.il/laws/special/eng/golan_heights_law.htm if needed.
